title_en: Book of Dhikr, Du'a, Repentance, and the Etiquette of the Quran
title_ar: كتاب الذكر والدعاء والتوبة وآداب القرآن
content_en:
The closing sections of Imam al-Nawawi's al-Minhaj Sharh Sahih Muslim address the spiritual dimensions of Islamic worship — dhikr (remembrance of Allah), du'a (supplication), tawbah (repentance), and the proper etiquette for engaging with the Quran. These chapters represent some of the most beloved portions of the entire commentary, as they deal with the inner life of the believer and the means by which one draws close to Allah.

The Virtue and Forms of Dhikr

Al-Nawawi begins by establishing the primacy of dhikr in the Islamic tradition. He cites the hadith of Abu Musa al-Ash'ari that the Prophet ﷺ said: "The parable of the one who remembers his Lord and the one who does not is like the living and the dead" (Sahih Muslim). Dhikr encompasses a broad range of remembrances: the recitation of Subhanallah, Alhamdulillah, Allahu Akbar, La ilaha illa Allah, Astaghfirullah, and Salawat upon the Prophet. Al-Nawawi explains that dhikr is not confined to specific words but includes any speech or action oriented toward pleasing Allah.

The commentary emphasizes that dhikr of the heart is superior to dhikr of the tongue alone, though the ideal is their combination. Al-Nawawi cites the Quranic injunction: "Remember your Lord much and glorify Him morning and evening" (Al-Ahzab 33:41-42). He notes that the scholars of tasawwuf (Islamic spirituality) built their entire methodology around the hadith literature on dhikr, understanding remembrance as the means of purifying the heart from attachment to other than Allah.

Conditions and Etiquette of Du'a

Al-Nawawi devotes considerable attention to the etiquette of supplication (adab al-du'a). He enumerates the conditions that increase the likelihood of acceptance: sincerity (ikhlas), certainty that Allah hears and responds, beginning with praise and salawat on the Prophet, facing the qiblah where possible, raising the hands, and maintaining a state of ritual purity. He also enumerates circumstances of heightened acceptance: the last third of the night, the time between the adhan and iqamah, while fasting, during rain, and in prostration.

The commentary addresses the theological question of why supplications are sometimes not answered as asked. Al-Nawawi cites the hadith that Allah either grants what is asked, stores it as reward for the Hereafter, or uses it to avert a harm. He emphasizes that the believer's trust in Allah's wisdom is itself a form of worship, and that persistence in du'a without despair is itself praiseworthy.

The Comprehensive Doctrine of Tawbah

Al-Nawawi's discussion of repentance (tawbah) is among the most systematic in classical Islamic literature. He establishes that tawbah is obligatory for every Muslim upon committing a sin and that it is accepted at any time prior to the sun rising from the west (a sign of the Hour) and prior to death. The conditions of valid tawbah are: ceasing the sin immediately, genuine remorse, firm resolve not to return, and — in the case of wronging another person — making restitution or seeking their pardon.

This chapter concludes with the etiquette of engaging with the Quran: maintaining ritual purity (wudu) is recommended though not obligatory for touching a copy of the Quran according to the majority opinion; recitation should be deliberate and unhurried (tartil); and reflection on the meanings (tadabbur) is the highest form of engagement with the divine text. Al-Nawawi closes with the hadith: "The best of you are those who learn the Quran and teach it" (Sahih al-Bukhari), affirming that engagement with the Quran is both a personal spiritual practice and a communal obligation.
